macro_segments.title: The Distinction Between Wounding and Medical Procedures: Blood-Letting and Thorn Removal

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Daf: The Definition of Makei Av Ve'imo as Requiring a Wound
The Yerushalmi's Debate on Whether a Permanent Wound or Any Blood Loss Suffices
The Bavli's Treatment of Medical Blood-Letting and Accidental Injury During Thorn Removal
Tosafos' Resolution: Linking Concern to Psik Reisha (Definite Consequence)
The Distinction Between Surgical Intent (Medical Context) and the Halacha's Apparent Concern
Extension of Concern Beyond Parents to Third Parties: The Parallel Problem
Redefining Injury by Context: The Role of Tikkun (Repair) on Shabbat
Resolution for Shabbat vs. Parent/Friend: Mekalkel Works for Shabbat, Not for Interpersonal Injury
Rabbi Shimon's Position: Wounding Is Inherently Mekalkel, Not Reliant on Intent
The Strange Conclusion: Medical Procedures Remain Problematic Despite Context

macro_segments.title: The Question of Court-Appointed Agency: Can a Son Serve as Shaliach Beis Din to Strike or Curse a Father?

micro_segments.title: Rav Sheshes' Question: Whether a Court Could Appoint a Son as Agent to Administer Lashes or Curse
The General Permission to Act as Court Agent: Distinguishing Between General License and Specific Mandate
The Kavod Shamayim Response: Defense of Heaven's Honor Overrides Parental Respect
The Mechilta's Derivation: Comparing Obligated Agents (Court Lash-Givers) with Prohibited Persons
Reading the Mechilta: Whether It Addresses Son vs. Third Party or General Prohibition on Hitting
Gemara's Clarification: The Mechilta Discusses General Prohibition, Not Parent-Specific Distinctions

macro_segments.title: The Case of One Led Out to Execution: When a Person Loses Protective Status (Gravra Katila)

micro_segments.title: The Scenario: A Son Hits and Curses a Father Being Led to Execution
Third Party Exemption: A Stranger Who Hits or Curses Is Exempt; a Son Remains Liable
The Question: What Explains the Difference Between Son and Third Party in This Context?
Rav Chisda's Answer: Resistance During Execution Justifies Force by Court Agent
Rav Sheshes' Reinterpretation: The Case Without Resistance; Gavra Katila Status
The Exemption from Monetary Payment for Damage to One Who Is Gavra Katila (Dead Man Walking)
Rabbi Ami on Minor Injuries: Hitting That Causes Less Than a Pruta's Damage Incurs Lashes, Not Payment
Reframing the Exemption: Exempt from Monetary Damages, Not from Transgression of Prohibition
The Principle of Oseh Ma'aseh Amcha: Non-Protection of Those Outside the Covenant Community
Cursing After Death vs. Hitting After Death: Why Cursing Remains Prohibited But Hitting Does Not
Rashi's Explanation: Blood as Nefesh (Life Force) Distinguishes Living Wounds from Post-Mortem Ones
The Son's Ongoing Liability Despite Gavra Katila Status: Why Medical/Biological Wound Still Applies

macro_segments.title: The Mishna's Distinctions and Derivations: Wounds, After-Death Liability, and the Oseh Ma'aseh Amcha Limitation

micro_segments.title: The Mishna: Wound Requirement for Makeh, Greater Severity of Kelalah Than Makeh
The Excess Repetition in the Parsha: Why 'Aviv V'Imo Kilel' Appears to Derive the After-Death Prohibition
Inferential Logic: Hitting Is Narrower (Life-Only), So Cursing (Also Life-Only) Should Not Apply After Death
Rebuttal: The Extra Repetition Overrides the Inference; Cursing Applies Even After Death
Rabbi Yoshiyah's Reading: The Double Language Teaches Cursing Either Parent Suffices
Rabbi Yonatan's Counter-Reading: The Vav Typically Requires Both Unless Specified Otherwise
The Question: Where Does the After-Death Application Come From?
The Braita's Byproduct: The Limitation of Cursation by the Oseh Ma'aseh Amcha Principle (Nasi B'Amcha)
The Gemara's Resolution: Using the Shemot Verse for After-Death, the Vayikra Verse for Multiple Perpetrators

macro_segments.title: The Mishna's Distinctions and Derivations: Wounds, After-Death Liability, and the Oseh Ma'aseh Amcha Limitation (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Braita's Question: Why Doesn't the Mishna Teach the Distinction Between Hitting (Broader) and Cursing (Narrower)?
Gemara's Answer: The Mishna's Author Holds That Hitting and Cursing Are Linked (Makkishin Hakah L'Kalalah)

macro_segments.title: The Oseh Ma'aseh Amcha Principle: Application to Non-Observant Jews and the Samaritan (Kuti) Debate

micro_segments.title: The First Braita on Samaritans (Kutim): Hitting Prohibited, Cursing Permitted
The Second Braita on Samaritans: Both Hitting and Cursing Permitted
Initial Assumption: The Braitas Debate Whether Samaritans Are Geirei Emet (Valid Converts) or Geirei Arayot (False Converts)
The Logic of Distinction: Cursing (Public Denunciation) vs. Hitting (Physical Injury) May Warrant Different Rules
Gemara's Refutation: The Reference to 'Alah Shor' Shows Both Braitas Treat Samaritans as Halachically Jewish
The Real Dispute: Both Agree Samaritans Are Jewish; They Dispute Whether Hitting Is Also Oseh Ma'aseh Amcha-Limited
One View: Hitting and Cursing Are Linked, So Both Fall Under Oseh Ma'aseh Amcha Limitation
Other View: Only Cursing Is Limited; Hitting Remains Prohibited Regardless of Observance
The Principle Introduced: Cursing Non-Observant Jews Does Not Transgress Based on Nasi B'Amcha
Conclusion: Same Principle Applies to Non-Observant Parents—Whether Hitting Is Also Oseh Ma'aseh Amcha-Limited Remains Debated
The Logical Question: If Cursing Is Limited to Oseh Ma'asch Amcha, Is Hitting Also Limited?
